metadata.title: Pitch Like Kawasaki: 10 Slides, 8 Minutes, 30-Point Font
metadata.playlist: Sell the Vision — Your Investor Pitch Video
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 7 brief — Video Pitch to Investors: the arc, Kawasaki 10/20/30, and 80/20 philosophy

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 1 of 7. Assignment 7 is the midterm: a recorded video pitch to investors, 100 points, 8 minutes hard cap. Most students have never pitched before. They treat it like a product demo or a class presentation. It is neither. An investor pitch has one job — make someone want to give you money to keep building. This series walks you through the exact structure that makes that happen: 10 slides, one per section, each with a job and a time budget. Episode 1 is the frame: why the 10/20/30 rule exists, what the 80/20 split means for your grade, who the audience is and what they actually care about, and the full 10-slide arc so you know where you are going before you write a single word.

props.segment: Pitch Like Kawasaki: 10 Slides, 8 Minutes, 30-Point Font
props.command: Scaffold my 10-slide pitch arc with a time budget for each slide — then run the madison-pitch intake prompt to draft all 10 slides from my Madison project.
props.runningText: the pitch has one job — make someone want to give you money. 10 slides, 8 minutes, 30-point font…
output: 10/20/30 rule: 10 slides · 8 min cap · 30pt font minimum
80/20: ship the full pitch cleanly = 80 pts (B) · memorable + investable = 20 more (A)
Audience: smart money, non-technical — cares about problems, solutions, and profits

beats.narration_text: The 10/20/30 rule comes from Guy Kawasaki, a venture capitalist who watched thousands of pitches and noticed three consistent failure modes. Too many slides: each extra slide dilutes the investor's attention and makes you look like you don't know what matters. Too long: the assignment caps you at 8 minutes — that is already more than most VC pitches get. And small fonts: when your font is small, you read off the slide instead of talking to the room. Reading off a slide kills credibility. It signals you are not confident enough in your own material to speak it. The 30-point minimum forces you to put fewer words on each slide, which forces you to know your material well enough to speak it. That is not a formatting rule — it is a practice discipline. The number the assignment uses is 8 minutes because you are a student, not a Series A founder. The Kawasaki original is 20 minutes for a sales call. The discipline is the same: know your content, stay in time, make every slide earn its place.

artifactLines: 10 slides max. Each extra slide dilutes attention and signals you don't know what matters.
8 minutes hard cap (assignment). Kawasaki original: 20 min. Discipline is the same — stay in time.
30-point font minimum. Forces you off the slide and into the room. Reading = no credibility.
Small font → read off slides → killed credibility. Big font → forced brevity → forced mastery.
The rule isn't about formatting. It's about knowing your material well enough to speak it.
props.sparkLine: 30-point font forces you to speak it, not read it.

beats.narration_text: The 80/20 split tells you exactly what you are competing for. Eighty points is a B. You earn it by shipping the full pitch professionally: clear problem, clear solution, 10 slides, under 8 minutes, no jargon, all slides present. That is the path most students need to focus on. The final 20 points — the A — are for a pitch that is memorable and investable: a problem the investor actually feels, a differentiator that holds up under a direct challenge, a business model that makes arithmetic sense. Here is what this means for how you spend your time. Do not chase the 20 before you have the 80. A pitch with polished production and a vague problem is worth 60 points — the production points cannot substitute for the content points. Build the 80 first: all 10 slides drafted, under 8 minutes, jargon-free. Then read it as if you are the investor asking the hard questions. The gaps you find after the 80 is solid are what earn the 20.

props.artifactHeading: Ship the 80% first — then chase the 20
artifactLines: 80 pts (B): all 10 slides · under 8 min · no jargon · clear problem and solution. That's it.
20 pts (A): memorable problem · differentiator that survives a challenge · arithmetic business model.
Warning: polished production + vague problem = 60 pts. Production can't substitute for content.
Order: draft all 10 slides → under 8 min → jargon-free → THEN read as the skeptical investor.
The gaps you find after the 80% is solid — those are the A points.
props.sparkLine: Don't chase the 20 before you have the 80.

beats.narration_text: The 10-slide arc with time budgets. Slide 1, the title: 15 seconds. Your name, your project name, one memorable line. Slide 2, the problem: 75 seconds. What is broken and why it matters — in numbers, not adjectives. Slide 3, the solution: 75 seconds. How you fix it, the aha in plain English. Slide 4, the magic: 45 seconds. What makes the technology work, in one sentence a seventh-grader understands. Slide 5, the business model: 50 seconds. How you make money — pricing, rough revenue estimate, labeled. Slide 6, go-to-market: 50 seconds. How you get the first 10 customers, specifically. Slide 7, competition: 45 seconds. Who else exists, why you win, your unfair advantage. Slide 8, development plan: 40 seconds. Where you are now and the milestones ahead. Slide 9, proof of concept: 75 seconds. Evidence you can build this — at whatever stage you are at. Slide 10, the ask: 30 seconds. What you want, from whom, by when. That is 500 seconds — 8 minutes and 20 seconds before rehearsal tightening. Practice cuts it under. Write that arc down. It is your master outline for every episode in this series.

artifactLines: S1 Title 15s · S2 Problem 75s · S3 Solution 75s · S4 Magic 45s · S5 Business 50s
S6 GTM 50s · S7 Competition 45s · S8 Dev Plan 40s · S9 POC 75s · S10 Ask 30s
Total: 500s = 8m 20s before rehearsal tightening. Practice cuts it under.
Episodes 2–5 cover slides 1–9 in pairs. Episode 6: recording. Episode 7: submit.
This arc is your master outline. Every episode in this series maps to it.

beats.act: who the audience is + introducing madison-pitch
beats.narration_text: Investors are not your professors. They are not grading your technical implementation. They are busy, successful business people — smart money that has seen hundreds of pitches and funded maybe ten percent of them. What they care about is four things: Is the problem real? Would people actually pay to have this fixed? Is the solution believable? Does this team have a credible path to execution? They do not care about n8n. They do not care about Python. They do not care about API rate limits. The moment you say 'our n8n workflow orchestrates multi-agent AI calls' you have lost them — not because they are not smart, but because you just told them you don't know how to translate your own work. The whole pitch must land for someone who has never heard of the tool. Test it on a family member. If they don't get it, rewrite it. This episode's tool is `madison-pitch`. It is a skill built for exactly this assignment: 10 slides, 8-minute timing check, jargon audit, skeptical-VC score. Every Your Turn prompt in this series routes through it.

props.artifactHeading: Smart money — non-technical, cares about problems and profits
artifactLines: They care: is the problem real? · is the solution believable? · is there a path to money?
They don't care: n8n · Python · API rate limits · your architecture diagram.
'Our n8n workflow orchestrates multi-agent AI calls' = you lost them. Translate everything.
Test: play the pitch for a family member. Every confused look is a rewrite, not a question.
Tool: `madison-pitch` — 10 slides, 8-min timing check, jargon audit, skeptical-VC score.
props.sparkLine: Translate your work. If a family member doesn't get it, rewrite it.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Episode 1. Three things to hold onto before Episode 2. One: 10 slides, 8 minutes, 30-point font — these are not preferences, they are the constraints that force a good pitch. Two: the 80/20 split. Get all 10 slides drafted, clear, and jargon-free before you worry about being memorable. The 80% path is: every slide present, under 8 minutes, a non-technical person understands it. Three: your audience is smart, non-technical money that cares about problems, solutions, and profits. The pitch does not describe your tool — it describes what the world looks like for a specific person after your tool exists. That is the translation that makes the difference between a cool project and a fundable pitch. Write the 10-slide arc with time budgets. That is your map for the rest of the series.

props.artifactTitle: Pitch Like Kawasaki: 10 Slides, 8 Minutes, 30-Point Font
artifactLines: 10/20/30: constraints that force mastery — not formatting preferences.
80/20: draft all 10 slides, under 8 min, jargon-free FIRST. Then chase the A.
Audience: smart money, non-technical — problems, solutions, profits. Not architecture.
The pitch describes the world after your tool — not the tool itself.
Your arc: 10 slides · 500 seconds · title through ask. Episodes 2–5 build it slide by slide.
Tool: `madison-pitch` runs intake → scaffolds all 10 slides from your Madison project.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude. Fill in the brackets before running. The prompt reads: Here is my Madison project: [paste a 3-to-5 sentence description of what your tool does, who it is for, and what problem it solves]. Run the madison-pitch intake to scaffold all 10 slides. For each slide: write the slide title, the one job this slide does in the pitch, a draft of the content in plain English with no technical jargon, and the time budget in seconds. Flag any slide where the content is vague or where you need more information from me to draft it. At the end, give me the total estimated time, and flag if any section is running over its budget. After the prompt runs: do you have 10 slides? Is the total time under 500 seconds? Are there any slides the prompt flagged as needing more information? Fill those in before Episode 2. If you do not have a madison-pitch skill available, skip the skill name and just run the prompt as written — it will still scaffold the arc. The full arc is what you need before the next episode.

props.segment: Pitch Like Kawasaki: 10 Slides, 8 Minutes, 30-Point Font
props.command: Here is my Madison project: [3–5 sentence description — what it does, who it's for, what problem it solves]. Run the madison-pitch intake: scaffold all 10 slides. For each slide: title · one job · plain-English draft (no jargon) · time budget in seconds. Flag vague slides. Give total estimated time. Flag anything over budget.
props.runningText: paste your Madison project description before running — the scaffold only works with specific product context…

beats.narration_text: Pitch Like Kawasaki: 10 Slides, 8 Minutes, 30-Point Font. Liam, in for Bear.
